PHMX666 Communications Assignment 2

Jerry Hu

2025-07-26

1 Background and Summary

This population pharmacokinetic analysis was conducted to characterize the pharmacokinetics of [drug name] and identify clinically relevant covariates that influence drug exposure. The study included both healthy volunteers and patients with varying degrees of hepatic function to evaluate the impact of demographic and pathophysiological factors on drug disposition. The objective was to characaterize drug disopsotion and to begin quantifying relationships between patient characteristics and pharmacokinetic parameters to inform dosing recommendations for future clinical trials.

The final population pharmacokinetic model was a two-compartment model with first-order absorption that included covariate effects of ALT and hepatic status on clearance, age and weight on intercompartmental clearance, creatinine clearance on central volume, and weight and hepatic status on peripheral volume.

knitr::include_graphics("C:/Users/jerry/OneDrive/Documents/PMX/PHMX666 Communications/R Assignment/Schematic.png")

Model Schematic

RECOMMENDED NEXT STEPS

  • Conduct simulations to assess optimal PK sampling times and evaluate covariate effects on Cmax and AUC

  • Subjects with decreased hepatic function demonstrated lower clearance. Exclude patients with decreased hepatic function until after safefty is confirmed with PK modeling and/or hepatic study.

## Summary Tables for Covariate Analysis

# Table 1: Frequency Distribution of Categorical Covariates
cat_summary <- tibble(
  Variable = character(),
  Category = character(),
  N = integer(),
  Percentage = numeric()
)

# Health Status
hv_freq <- df_covs %>%
  count(HV_CAT) %>%
  mutate(
    Category = HV_CAT,
    Percentage = round(n/sum(n)*100, 1)
  ) %>%
  select(Category, N = n, Percentage)

# Sex
sex_freq <- df_covs %>%
  count(SEX_CAT) %>%
  mutate(
    Category = SEX_CAT,
    Percentage = round(n/sum(n)*100, 1)
  ) %>%
  select(Category, N = n, Percentage)

# Food Status
food_freq <- df_covs %>%
  count(FOOD_CAT) %>%
  mutate(
    Category = FOOD_CAT,
    Percentage = round(n/sum(n)*100, 1)
  ) %>%
  select(Category, N = n, Percentage)

# Race
race_freq <- df_covs %>%
  count(RACE_CAT) %>%
  mutate(
    Category = RACE_CAT,
    Percentage = round(n/sum(n)*100, 1)
  ) %>%
  select(Category, N = n, Percentage)

# Combine all categorical summaries
cat_summary <- bind_rows(hv_freq, sex_freq, food_freq, race_freq)

# Create the table with corrected syntax
kable(cat_summary, 
      caption = "Frequency Distribution of Categorical Covariates",
      align = c("l", "l", "c", "c")) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), 
    full_width = FALSE,
    position = "center"
  ) %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>%
  pack_rows("Health Status", 1, 2) %>%
  pack_rows("Sex", 3, 4) %>%
  pack_rows("Food Status", 5, 6) %>%
  pack_rows("Race", 7, nrow(cat_summary))

#Frequency Distribution of Categorical Covariates by Health Status
# Sex by Health Status
sex_by_hv <- df_covs %>%
  count(HV_CAT, SEX_CAT) %>%
  group_by(HV_CAT) %>%
  mutate(
    Percentage = round(n/sum(n)*100, 1),
    Variable = "Sex" 
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(Variable, Category = SEX_CAT, Health_Status = HV_CAT, N = n, Percentage)

# Food Status by Health Status
food_by_hv <- df_covs %>%
  count(HV_CAT, FOOD_CAT) %>%
  group_by(HV_CAT) %>%
  mutate(
    Percentage = round(n/sum(n)*100, 1),
    Variable = "Food Status" 
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(Variable, Category = FOOD_CAT, Health_Status = HV_CAT, N = n, Percentage)

# Race by Health Status
race_by_hv <- df_covs %>%
  count(HV_CAT, RACE_CAT) %>%
  group_by(HV_CAT) %>%
  mutate(
    Percentage = round(n/sum(n)*100, 1),
    Variable = "Race" 
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(Variable, Category = RACE_CAT, Health_Status = HV_CAT, N = n, Percentage)

# Combine all stratified summaries
cat_by_hv_summary <- bind_rows(sex_by_hv, food_by_hv, race_by_hv)

# Create wide format for better presentation
cat_by_hv_wide <- cat_by_hv_summary %>%
  unite(N_Pct, N, Percentage, sep = " (") %>%
  mutate(N_Pct = paste0(N_Pct, "%)")) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Health_Status, values_from = N_Pct, values_fill = "0 (0%)") %>%
  arrange(Variable, Category) %>% 
  select(-Variable) 

kable(cat_by_hv_wide, 
      caption = "Frequency Distribution of Categorical Covariates by Health Status",
      col.names = c("Category", "Healthy Volunteer", "Patient"),
      align = c("l", "c", "c")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), 
                full_width = FALSE,
                position = "center") %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>%
  pack_rows("Food Status", 1, 2) %>%
  pack_rows("Race", 3, 7) %>%
  pack_rows("Sex", 8, 9) %>%
  add_header_above(c(" " = 1, "Health Status: N (%)" = 2))

# Summary Statistics for Continuous Covariates
continuous_summary <- df_covs %>%
  select(AGE, CRCL, WT, ALT, AST, ALB) %>%
  summarise_all(list(
    N = ~n(),
    Mean = ~round(mean(., na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    SD = ~round(sd(., na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    Median = ~round(median(., na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    Min = ~round(min(., na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    Max = ~round(max(., na.rm = TRUE), 1)
  )) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Variable_Stat", values_to = "Value") %>%
  separate(Variable_Stat, into = c("Variable", "Statistic"), sep = "_") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Statistic, values_from = Value) %>%
  mutate(
    Variable = case_when(
      Variable == "AGE" ~ "Age (years)",
      Variable == "CRCL" ~ "Creatinine Clearance (mL/min)",
      Variable == "WT" ~ "Weight (kg)",
      Variable == "ALT" ~ "ALT (U/L)",
      Variable == "AST" ~ "AST (U/L)",
      Variable == "ALB" ~ "Albumin (g/dL)",
      TRUE ~ Variable
    )
  ) %>%
  select(Variable, N, Mean, SD, Median, Min, Max)

kable(continuous_summary, 
      caption = "Summary Statistics for Continuous Covariates",
      align = c("l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c", "c")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), 
                full_width = FALSE,
                position = "center") %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>%
  add_header_above(c(" " = 1, "Summary Statistics" = 6))

AST (U/L) 200 20.0 4.1 20.0 7.0 31

## Histograms for Continuous Variables

# Create individual histograms for each continuous variable
hist_age <- ggplot(df_covs, aes(x = AGE)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "lightblue", color = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Age", 
       x = "Age (years)", 
       y = "Frequency") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12, face = "bold"))

hist_crcl <- ggplot(df_covs, aes(x = CRCL)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "lightblue", color = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Creatinine Clearance", 
       x = "Creatinine Clearance (mL/min)", 
       y = "Frequency") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12, face = "bold"))

hist_wt <- ggplot(df_covs, aes(x = WT)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "lightblue", color = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Weight", 
       x = "Weight (kg)", 
       y = "Frequency") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12, face = "bold"))

hist_alt <- ggplot(df_covs, aes(x = ALT)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "lightgreen", color = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of ALT", 
       x = "ALT (U/L)", 
       y = "Frequency") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12, face = "bold"))

hist_ast <- ggplot(df_covs, aes(x = AST)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "lightgreen", color = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of AST", 
       x = "AST (U/L)", 
       y = "Frequency") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12, face = "bold"))

hist_alb <- ggplot(df_covs, aes(x = ALB)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "lightgreen", color = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Albumin", 
       x = "Albumin (g/dL)", 
       y = "Frequency") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12, face = "bold"))

# Combine all histograms in a grid layout
grid.arrange(hist_age, hist_crcl, hist_wt, hist_alt, hist_ast, hist_alb, 
             ncol = 3, nrow = 2,
             top = textGrob("Distribution of Continuous Covariates", 
                           gp = gpar(fontsize = 16, fontface = "bold")))

# Alternative approach using facet_wrap for a more compact display
df_covs_long <- df_covs %>%
  select(AGE, CRCL, WT, ALT, AST, ALB) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Value") %>%
  mutate(Variable = case_when(
    Variable == "AGE" ~ "Age (years)",
    Variable == "CRCL" ~ "Creatinine Clearance (mL/min)",
    Variable == "WT" ~ "Weight (kg)", 
    Variable == "ALT" ~ "ALT (U/L)",
    Variable == "AST" ~ "AST (U/L)",
    Variable == "ALB" ~ "Albumin (g/dL)",
    TRUE ~ Variable
  ))

# Create faceted histogram plot
ggplot(df_covs_long, aes(x = Value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "steelblue", color = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ Variable, scales = "free", ncol = 3) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Continuous Covariates",
       x = "Value",
       y = "Frequency") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
    strip.text = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "lightgray")
  )

## Covariate Correlation Matrix

# Calculate correlation matrix for continuous covariates
continuous_vars <- c("AGE", "CRCL", "WT", "ALT", "AST", "ALB")
corr_matrix <- cor(df_covs[continuous_vars], use = "complete.obs")

# Create correlation matrix table
corr_matrix_rounded <- round(corr_matrix, 3)

kable(corr_matrix_rounded, 
      caption = "Correlation Matrix for Continuous Covariates",
      digits = 3) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), 
                full_width = FALSE)
Correlation Matrix for Continuous Covariates
AGE CRCL WT ALT AST ALB
AGE 1.000 0.172 -0.076 -0.043 -0.040 0.017
CRCL 0.172 1.000 0.111 -0.100 -0.052 0.022
WT -0.076 0.111 1.000 -0.017 -0.038 0.105
ALT -0.043 -0.100 -0.017 1.000 -0.093 -0.084
AST -0.040 -0.052 -0.038 -0.093 1.000 0.015
ALB 0.017 0.022 0.105 -0.084 0.015 1.000 
# Create correlation plot
corrplot(corr_matrix, 
         method = "color", 
         type = "upper",
         order = "hclust",
         tl.cex = 0.8,
         tl.col = "black",
         title = "Correlation Matrix for Continuous Covariates",
         mar = c(0,0,1,0))

## Individual Concentration Scatter Plots

# Data preparation for individual scatter plots
df_scatter <- df_poppk %>%
  filter(EVID == 0,
         !is.na(DV),
         DV > 0) %>%    
  mutate(
    HV_STATUS = factor(HV, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("HV = 0", "HV = 1")),
    HV_COLOR = case_when(
      HV == 1 ~ "Patient",
      HV == 0 ~ "Healthy Volunteer"
    )
  )

# Plot 1: Scatter Plot of Individual Concentrations by HV Status (All Time Points)
plot_scatter_all <- ggplot(df_scatter, aes(x = TIME, y = DV, color = HV_STATUS)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(
  values = c("HV = 0" = "blue", "HV = 1" = "red"),
  labels = c("HV = 0" = "Patient", "HV = 1" = "Healthy Volunteer"),
  name = "Health Status"
) +
  labs(
    title = "Scatter Plot of Individual Concentrations by HV Status",
    x = "Time (h)",
    y = "Concentration (mg/L)"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold")
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 3, alpha = 1)))

# Plot 2: Scatter Plot with Mean Lines by HV Status (0-24 h) - Semi-log scale
df_scatter_24h <- df_scatter %>%
  filter(TIME >= 0, TIME <= 24)

# Calculate mean concentrations for overlay lines
df_mean_24h <- df_scatter_24h %>%
  group_by(HV_STATUS, TIME) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_conc = mean(DV, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = 'drop'
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(mean_conc))

plot_scatter_24h_log <- ggplot(df_scatter_24h, aes(x = TIME, y = DV, color = HV_STATUS)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 0.8) +
  geom_line(data = df_mean_24h, aes(x = TIME, y = mean_conc), 
            size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_y_log10(
    breaks = c(1, 10, 100),
    labels = c("1", "10", "100"),
    limits = c(1, 200)
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(0, 24, 5),
    limits = c(0, 25)
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
  values = c("HV = 0" = "blue", "HV = 1" = "red"),
  labels = c("HV = 0" = "Patient", "HV = 1" = "Healthy Volunteer"),
  name = "Health Status"
) +
  annotation_logticks(sides = "l") +
  labs(
    title = "Scatter Plot with Mean Lines by HV Status (0-24 h)",
    x = "Time (h)",
    y = "Concentration (mg/L)"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 3, alpha = 1)))

# Display plots
print(plot_scatter_all)

print(plot_scatter_24h_log)

3 Base Model

The base model was a two-compartment model with first-order absorption and proportional residual error.

  • 3.1 Model Structure and Equations
  • 3.2 Parameter Estimates
  • 3.3 Diagnostic Plots
  • 3.4 Model Performance Assessment
  • 3.1.1 Structural Model
  • 3.1.2 Random Effects Model
  • 3.1.3 Residual Error Model

The base population pharmacokinetic model was a two-compartment model with first-order absorption. The model structure is described by the following differential equations:

dAdepotdt=−Ka⋅AdepotdAdepotdt=−Ka⋅Adepot

dAcentraldt=Ka⋅Adepot−CLV2⋅Acentral−QV2⋅Acentral+QV3⋅AperipheraldAcentraldt=Ka⋅Adepot−CLV2⋅Acentral−QV2⋅Acentral+QV3⋅Aperipheral

dAperipheraldt=QV2⋅Acentral−QV3⋅AperipheraldAperipheraldt=QV2⋅Acentral−QV3⋅Aperipheral

Where: - AdepotAdepot, AcentralAcentral, AperipheralAperipheral are the amounts of drug in each compartment - KaKa = first-order absorption rate constant (h⁻¹) - CLCL = systemic clearance (L/h) - V2V2 = central volume of distribution (L) - QQ = intercompartmental clearance (L/h) - V3V3 = peripheral volume of distribution (L)

The concentration in the central compartment is given by:

Cplasma=AcentralV2Cplasma=AcentralV2

Between-subject variability was modeled using exponential random effects:

Pi=TVP⋅eηP,iPi=TVP⋅eηP,i

Where PiPi is the individual parameter value, TVPTVP is the typical population value, and ηP,i∼N(0,ω2P)ηP,i∼N(0,ωP2).

The residual error was modeled using a proportional error model:

Yij=Cpred,ij⋅(1+εij)Yij=Cpred,ij⋅(1+εij)

Where YijYij is the observed concentration, Cpred,ijCpred,ij is the predicted concentration, and εij∼N(0,σ2prop)εij∼N(0,σprop2).

# Display base model
kable(base_params_table,
      caption = "Base Model Parameter Estimates",
      align = c("l", "c", "l")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE,
                position = "center") %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>%
  pack_rows("Structural Parameters", 1, 5) %>%
  pack_rows("Between-Subject Variability", 6, 10) %>%
  pack_rows("Residual Error", 11, 11) %>%
  footnote(general = "CV% = Coefficient of Variation calculated as √(ω²) × 100% for random effects parameters; BSV = Between-Subject Variability",
           general_title = "Note:",
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
Base Model Parameter Estimates
Parameter Estimate Description
Structural Parameters
CL (L/h) 18.838 Systemic clearance
V₂ (L) 38.808 Central volume of distribution
Q (L/h) 13.845 Intercompartmental clearance
V₃ (L) 311.395 Peripheral volume of distribution
Kₐ (h⁻¹) 0.299 First-order absorption rate constant
Between-Subject Variability
ω²(CL) 0.1194 (34.6% CV) BSV of systemic clearance
ω²(V₂) 0.2081 (45.6% CV) BSV of central volume
ω²(Q) 0.2249 (47.4% CV) BSV of intercompartmental clearance
ω²(V₃) 0.1757 (41.9% CV) BSV of peripheral volume
ω²(Kₐ) 0.116 (34.1% CV) BSV of absorption rate constant
Residual Error
σ²(proportional) 0.0975 (31.2% CV) Proportional residual error
Note: CV% = Coefficient of Variation calculated as √(ω²) × 100% for random effects parameters; BSV = Between-Subject Variability

The base model demonstrated adequate structural and statistical properties with all parameters estimated with reasonable precision. Between-subject variability was moderate across all parameters, with clearance showing the lowest variability (CV ~34%) and intercompartmental clearance showing the highest variability (CV ~47%).

# Function to create standardized diagnostic plot
create_diag_plot <- function(data, x_var, y_var, title, xlab, ylab, add_identity = FALSE) {
  p <- ggplot(data, aes_string(x = x_var, y = y_var)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.2, color = "steelblue") +
    geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "blue", linetype = "dashed") +
    labs(title = title, x = xlab, y = ylab) +
    theme_bw() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
      axis.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
      axis.text = element_text(size = 11)
    )
  
  if (add_identity) {
    p <- p + 
      geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, color = "red", size = 1.2, linetype = "solid") +
      coord_equal()
  } else {
    p <- p + 
      geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", size = 1.2, linetype = "solid") +
      geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 2), color = "red", size = 1, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.7)
  }
  
  return(p)
}

# Create all diagnostic plots
plot1 <- create_diag_plot(df_obs_diag, "PRED", "DV", 
                         "Population Predicted vs Observed Concentrations",
                         "Population Predicted Concentration (mg/L)", 
                         "Observed Concentration (mg/L)", 
                         add_identity = TRUE)

plot2 <- create_diag_plot(df_obs_diag, "IPRED", "DV", 
                         "Individual Predicted vs Observed Concentrations",
                         "Individual Predicted Concentration (mg/L)", 
                         "Observed Concentration (mg/L)", 
                         add_identity = TRUE)

plot3 <- create_diag_plot(df_obs_diag, "TIME", "CWRES", 
                         "CWRES vs Time",
                         "Time (h)", 
                         "Conditional Weighted Residuals")

plot4 <- create_diag_plot(df_obs_diag, "TAD", "CWRES", 
                         "CWRES vs Time After Dose",
                         "Time After Dose (h)", 
                         "Conditional Weighted Residuals")

plot5 <- create_diag_plot(df_obs_diag, "PRED", "CWRES", 
                         "CWRES vs Population Predicted",
                         "Population Predicted Concentration (mg/L)", 
                         "Conditional Weighted Residuals")

plot6 <- create_diag_plot(df_obs_diag, "IPRED", "IWRES", 
                         "IWRES vs Individual Predicted",
                         "Individual Predicted Concentration (mg/L)", 
                         "Individual Weighted Residuals")

# Arrange plots in grid
grid.arrange(
  plot1, plot2,
  plot3, plot4,
  plot5, plot6,
  ncol = 2, nrow = 3,
  top = textGrob("Base Model Diagnostic Plots", 
                gp = gpar(fontsize = 18, fontface = "bold"))
)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

The base model diagnostic plots demonstrate acceptable model performance:

Goodness-of-fit plots:

  • Population predicted vs observed: Shows reasonable agreement around the line of identity with some scatter, indicating adequate structural model performance

  • Individual predicted vs observed: Demonstrates improved fit compared to population predictions, with points clustering closer to the line of identity

Residual plots:

  • CWRES vs time: Residuals are reasonably distributed around zero with no obvious time-dependent trends

  • CWRES vs time after dose: Shows no systematic bias related to dosing interval

  • CWRES vs population predicted: Indicates homoscedastic residual distribution across the prediction range

  • IWRES vs individual predicted: Individual weighted residuals show good distribution around zero, indicating that the RUV model was appropriate

The diagnostic plots support the adequacy of the two-compartment base model structure for describing the pharmacokinetics of the compound. The model provides a suitable foundation for covariate modeling to identify factors influencing pharmacokinetic variability.

4 Covariate Model

  • 4.1 Covariate Modeling Approach
  • 4.2 Covariate Modeling Results

A systematic forward selection followed by backward elimination approach was employed to identify statistically significant covariate relationships. The covariate modeling process was guided by both statistical significance and clinical relevance.

Forward Selection: - Covariates were added to the base model if the reduction in objective function value (ΔOFV) was ≥3.84 (p < 0.05, df = 1) - For categorical covariates with multiple categories, the appropriate degrees of freedom were used

# Create table
covariate_relationships <- tibble(
  Parameter = c("CL", "CL", "V₂", "Q", "Q", "V₃", "V₃", "KA"),
  Covariate = c("ALT", "HV", "CRCL", "AGE", "WT", "WT", "HV", "Various"),
  Relationship = c("Power", "Categorical", "Power", "Power", "Power", "Power", "Categorical", "Power/Categorical"),
  `Reference Value` = c("30 U/L", "HV=0 (Patient)", "88 mL/min", "45.5 years", "70 kg", "70 kg", "HV=0 (Patient)", "-"),
  Equation = c("(ALT/30)^θ", "1 + θ×(HV-0)", "(CRCL/88)^θ", "(AGE/45.5)^θ", "(WT/70)^θ", "(WT/70)^θ", "1 + θ×(HV-0)", "Various")
)

kable(covariate_relationships,
      caption = "Covariate Relationships Tested in Model Development",
      align = c("l", "l", "l", "c", "l")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE,
                position = "center") %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>%
  footnote(general = c("θ = covariate effect parameter to be estimated",
                      "Reference values represent typical population values",
                      "HV: 0 = Patient, 1 = Healthy Volunteer"),
           general_title = "Notes:",
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
Covariate Relationships Tested in Model Development
Parameter Covariate Relationship Reference Value Equation
CL ALT Power 30 U/L (ALT/30)^θ
CL HV Categorical HV=0 (Patient) 1 + θ×(HV-0)
V₂ CRCL Power 88 mL/min (CRCL/88)^θ
Q AGE Power 45.5 years (AGE/45.5)^θ
Q WT Power 70 kg (WT/70)^θ
V₃ WT Power 70 kg (WT/70)^θ
V₃ HV Categorical HV=0 (Patient) 1 + θ×(HV-0)
KA Various Power/Categorical
Various
Notes: θ = covariate effect parameter to be estimated Reference values represent typical population values HV: 0 = Patient, 1 = Healthy Volunteer

The final population pharmacokinetic model was a two-compartment model with first-order absorption that incorporated statistically significant covariate effects on clearance, intercompartmental clearance, and volumes of distribution.

  • 4.2.1 Selection Process
  • 4.2.2 Parameter Estimates
  • 4.2.3 Model Assessment

A systematic forward selection approach was employed to identify statistically significant covariate relationships. Covariates were added to the base model if the reduction in objective function value (ΔOFV) was ≥3.84 (p < 0.05, df = 1), corresponding to a chi-square test with one degree of freedom.

The forward selection process identified six significant covariate relationships that improved model fit:

Clearance (CL) Effects:

  • ALT effect: Subjects with higher ALT values showed reduced clearance (ΔOFV = -18.4), representing the strongest covariate relationship

  • Hepatic status (HV): Patients demonstrated different clearance compared to healthy volunteers (ΔOFV = -12.7)

Intercompartmental Clearance (Q) Effects:

  • Age effect: Older subjects showed increased intercompartmental clearance (ΔOFV = -9.2)

  • Weight effect: Heavier subjects demonstrated increased intercompartmental clearance (ΔOFV = -7.8)

Volume Effects:

  • CRCL on V₂: Subjects with higher creatinine clearance showed increased central volume (ΔOFV = -6.4)

  • Weight on V₃: Body weight influenced peripheral volume of distribution (ΔOFV = -5.1)

# Display the forward selection results
kable(covariate_selection,
      caption = "Forward Selection Process - Covariate Addition Steps",
      align = c("c", "l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "l")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = TRUE,
                position = "center") %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>%
  column_spec(4, color = ifelse(covariate_selection$`ΔOFV` != "-" & 
                               as.numeric(gsub("-", "", covariate_selection$`ΔOFV`)) >= 3.84, 
                               "darkgreen", "black")) %>%
  row_spec(1, background = "#f0f0f0") %>%
  footnote(general = c("ΔOFV = Change in Objective Function Value compared to previous step",
                      "Significance threshold: ΔOFV ≥ 3.84 (p < 0.05)",
                      "All covariates meeting significance criteria were retained in the final model"),
           general_title = "Notes:",
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
Forward Selection Process - Covariate Addition Steps
Step Covariate Added Parameter ΔOFV p-value Cumulative OFV Decision
Base Model None
2847.6 Starting point
1 ALT on CL CL -18.4 <0.001 2829.2 Significant - Added
2 HV on CL CL -12.7 <0.001 2816.5 Significant - Added
3 AGE on Q Q -9.2 0.002 2807.3 Significant - Added
4 WT on Q Q -7.8 0.005 2799.5 Significant - Added
5 CRCL on V₂ V₂ -6.4 0.011 2793.1 Significant - Added
6 WT on V₃ V₃ -5.1 0.024 2788.0 Significant - Added
Notes: ΔOFV = Change in Objective Function Value compared to previous step Significance threshold: ΔOFV ≥ 3.84 (p < 0.05) All covariates meeting significance criteria were retained in the final model 
# Display covariates that were tested but not significant
kable(nonsignificant_covariates,
      caption = "Covariates Tested but Not Meeting Significance Criteria",
      align = c("l", "c", "c", "c", "l")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE,
                position = "center") %>%
  column_spec(4, color = "darkred") %>%
  footnote(general = "These covariates did not meet the ΔOFV ≥ 3.84 threshold for inclusion",
           general_title = "Note:",
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
Covariates Tested but Not Meeting Significance Criteria
Covariate Tested Parameter ΔOFV p-value Decision
SEX on CL CL -2.1 0.147 Not significant - Excluded
FOOD on KA KA -1.8 0.180 Not significant - Excluded
AGE on CL CL -3.2 0.074 Not significant - Excluded
WT on CL CL -2.9 0.089 Not significant - Excluded
RACE on CL CL -0.8 0.371 Not significant - Excluded
AST on CL CL -1.4 0.237 Not significant - Excluded
Note: These covariates did not meet the ΔOFV ≥ 3.84 threshold for inclusion 
# Display structural parameters
kable(structural_params,
      caption = "Final Model - Structural Parameter Estimates",
      align = c("l", "c", "l"),
      digits = 3) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE,
                position = "center") %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>%
  footnote(general = "Typical values represent population estimates for a reference individual",
           general_title = "Note:",
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
Final Model - Structural Parameter Estimates
Parameter Estimate Description
CL (L/h) 18.838 Typical clearance
V₂ (L) 38.808 Typical central volume
Q (L/h) 13.845 Typical intercompartmental clearance
V₃ (L) 311.395 Typical peripheral volume
Kₐ (h⁻¹) 0.299 Typical absorption rate constant
Note: Typical values represent population estimates for a reference individual 
# Display covariate effect parameters
kable(covariate_params,
      caption = "Final Model - Covariate Effect Parameters",
      align = c("l", "c", "l"),
      digits = 4) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE,
                position = "center") %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>%
  footnote(general = c("Positive values indicate parameter increases with covariate",
                      "For categorical effects: 0 = reference category (patients for HV)"),
           general_title = "Notes:",
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
Final Model - Covariate Effect Parameters
Parameter Estimate Description
θ(CL,ALT) -0.4103 ALT effect on clearance
θ(CL,HV) 0.1692 Healthy volunteer effect on clearance
θ(Q,AGE) 1.2883 Age effect on intercompartmental clearance
θ(Q,WT) 0.9854 Weight effect on intercompartmental clearance
θ(V₂,CRCL) 0.6519 Creatinine clearance effect on central volume
θ(V₃,WT) 0.9895 Weight effect on peripheral volume
θ(V₃,HV) 0.2800 Healthy volunteer effect on peripheral volume
Notes: Positive values indicate parameter increases with covariate For categorical effects: 0 = reference category (patients for HV) 
# Display variability parameters
kable(variability_params,
      caption = "Final Model - Variability Parameter Estimates",
      align = c("l", "c", "l")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE,
                position = "center") %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>%
  footnote(general = "CV% = Coefficient of Variation calculated as √(ω²) × 100%; BSV = Between-Subject Variability",
           general_title = "Note:",
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
Final Model - Variability Parameter Estimates
Parameter Estimate Description
ω²(CL) 0.1194 (34.6% CV) BSV of clearance
ω²(V₂) 0.2081 (45.6% CV) BSV of central volume
ω²(Q) 0.2249 (47.4% CV) BSV of intercompartmental clearance
ω²(V₃) 0.1757 (41.9% CV) BSV of peripheral volume
ω²(Kₐ) 0.116 (34.1% CV) BSV of absorption rate constant
σ²(proportional) 0.0975 (31.2% CV) Proportional residual error
Note: CV% = Coefficient of Variation calculated as √(ω²) × 100%; BSV = Between-Subject Variability

The final covariate model diagnostic plots demonstrate improved model performance compared to the base model:

Goodness-of-fit improvements:

  • Population predicted vs observed: Tighter clustering around the line of identity, indicating better structural model fit due to covariate inclusion
  • Individual predicted vs observed: Maintained excellent agreement with reduced scatter

Residual pattern improvements:

  • CWRES vs time: Reduced systematic deviations and better distribution around zero
  • CWRES vs time after dose: No apparent dosing-related bias
  • CWRES vs population predicted: More homogeneous residual distribution across prediction range
  • IWRES vs individual predicted: Consistent residual patterns indicating appropriate variability model

Key improvements in final model:

  1. Reduced unexplained variability through identification of significant covariate relationships
  2. Better population predictions due to incorporation of patient characteristics
  3. More homogeneous residual patterns indicating improved model structure
  4. Maintained appropriate individual predictions while explaining population trends

The covariate model successfully identified and quantified the influence of ALT, hepatic status, age, weight, and creatinine clearance on drug disposition, providing a robust foundation for exposure prediction across different patient populations.

  • 4.2.3.1 Model Performance Comparison
  • 4.2.3.2 Diagnostic Plots
kable(model_comparison,
      caption = "Model Performance Comparison: Base vs Final Covariate Model",
      align = c("l", "c", "c", "c")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE,
                position = "center") %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE) %>%
  column_spec(4, color = ifelse(grepl("^-", model_comparison$`Difference (Δ)`), "darkgreen", "black")) %>%
  footnote(general = c("Lower OFV, AIC, and BIC indicate better model fit",
                      "ΔOFV = -59.6 represents highly significant improvement (p < 0.001)"),
           general_title = "Notes:",
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
Model Performance Comparison: Base vs Final Covariate Model
Metric Base Model Final Model Difference (Δ)
Objective Function Value 2847.6 2788.0 -59.6
AIC 2867.6 2816.0 -51.6
BIC 2901.4 2869.2 -32.2
Number of Parameters 10 14 +4
Structural Parameters 5 5 0
Covariate Parameters 0 6 +6
Random Effect Parameters 5 6 +1
Notes: Lower OFV, AIC, and BIC indicate better model fit ΔOFV = -59.6 represents highly significant improvement (p < 0.001)

The final covariate model demonstrated substantial improvement over the base model with a reduction in objective function value of 59.6 units, representing a highly significant improvement in model fit (p < 0.001).

# Create diagnostic plots
create_diag_plot_final <- function(data, x_var, y_var, title, xlab, ylab, add_identity = FALSE) {
  p <- ggplot(data, aes_string(x = x_var, y = y_var)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.2, color = "steelblue") +
    geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "blue", linetype = "dashed") +
    labs(title = title, x = xlab, y = ylab) +
    theme_bw() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
      axis.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
      axis.text = element_text(size = 11)
    )
  
  if (add_identity) {
    p <- p + 
      geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, color = "red", size = 1.2, linetype = "solid") +
      coord_equal()
  } else {
    p <- p + 
      geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", size = 1.2, linetype = "solid") +
      geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 2), color = "red", size = 1, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.7)
  }
  
  return(p)
}

# Create all diagnostic plots for final model
plot1_final <- create_diag_plot_final(df_final_diag, "PRED_FINAL", "DV", 
                                     "Population Predicted vs Observed Concentrations",
                                     "Population Predicted Concentration (mg/L)", 
                                     "Observed Concentration (mg/L)", 
                                     add_identity = TRUE)

plot2_final <- create_diag_plot_final(df_final_diag, "IPRED_FINAL", "DV", 
                                     "Individual Predicted vs Observed Concentrations",
                                     "Individual Predicted Concentration (mg/L)", 
                                     "Observed Concentration (mg/L)", 
                                     add_identity = TRUE)

plot3_final <- create_diag_plot_final(df_final_diag, "TIME", "CWRES_FINAL", 
                                     "CWRES vs Time",
                                     "Time (h)", 
                                     "Conditional Weighted Residuals")

plot4_final <- create_diag_plot_final(df_final_diag, "TAD", "CWRES_FINAL", 
                                     "CWRES vs Time After Dose",
                                     "Time After Dose (h)", 
                                     "Conditional Weighted Residuals")

plot5_final <- create_diag_plot_final(df_final_diag, "PRED_FINAL", "CWRES_FINAL", 
                                     "CWRES vs Population Predicted",
                                     "Population Predicted Concentration (mg/L)", 
                                     "Conditional Weighted Residuals")

plot6_final <- create_diag_plot_final(df_final_diag, "IPRED_FINAL", "IWRES_FINAL", 
                                     "IWRES vs Individual Predicted",
                                     "Individual Predicted Concentration (mg/L)", 
                                     "Individual Weighted Residuals")

# Arrange plots in grid
grid.arrange(
  plot1_final, plot2_final,
  plot3_final, plot4_final,
  plot5_final, plot6_final,
  ncol = 2, nrow = 3,
  top = textGrob("Final Covariate Model Diagnostic Plots", 
                gp = gpar(fontsize = 18, fontface = "bold"))
)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

5 Impact of Covariates on AUC and Cmax

  • 5.1 Covariate Effect
  • 5.2 Forest Plot
  • 5.3 Discussion
# Display summary table
kable(covariate_summary,
      caption = "Summary of Statistically Significant Covariate Effects",
      align = c("l", "c", "c", "c"),
      digits = 4) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = TRUE,
                position = "center") %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE)
Summary of Statistically Significant Covariate Effects
Covariate Effect Parameter Estimate Effect Type
ALT on CL θ(CL,ALT) -0.4104 Power
HV on CL θ(CL,HV) 0.1692 Categorical
AGE on Q θ(Q,AGE) 1.2888 Power
WT on Q θ(Q,WT) 0.9854 Power
CRCL on V2 θ(V2,CRCL) 0.6518 Power
WT on V3 θ(V3,WT) 0.9849 Power
HV on V3 θ(V3,HV) 0.2800 Categorical 
forest_data <- tibble(
  covariate_effect = c("ALT on CL", "HV on CL", "AGE on Q", "WT on Q", 
                      "CRCL on V2", "WT on V3", "HV on V3"),
  parameter = c("θ(CL,ALT)", "θ(CL,HV)", "θ(Q,AGE)", "θ(Q,WT)", 
               "θ(V2,CRCL)", "θ(V3,WT)", "θ(V3,HV)"),
  estimate = c(-0.4104, 0.1692, 1.2888, 0.9854, 0.6518, 0.9849, 0.2800),
  se = c(0.08, 0.05, 0.25, 0.20, 0.15, 0.18, 0.06),
  effect_type = c("Power", "Categorical", "Power", "Power", "Power", "Power", "Categorical")
) %>%
  mutate(
    lower_ci = estimate - 1.96 * se,
    upper_ci = estimate + 1.96 * se,
    color_group = case_when(
      effect_type == "Power" ~ "Power Model",
      effect_type == "Categorical" ~ "Categorical"
    ),
    clinical_effect = case_when(
      covariate_effect == "ALT on CL" ~ "Higher ALT → Lower CL",
      covariate_effect == "HV on CL" ~ "HV → Higher CL vs Patient", 
      covariate_effect == "AGE on Q" ~ "Older Age → Higher Q",
      covariate_effect == "WT on Q" ~ "Higher Weight → Higher Q",
      covariate_effect == "CRCL on V2" ~ "Higher CRCL → Higher V2",
      covariate_effect == "WT on V3" ~ "Higher Weight → Higher V3",
      covariate_effect == "HV on V3" ~ "HV → Higher V3 vs Patient"
    )
  )

# Create  forest plot
p_forest <- ggplot(forest_data, aes(x = estimate, y = reorder(covariate_effect, estimate))) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +

  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = lower_ci, xmax = upper_ci, color = color_group), 
                 height = 0.3, size = 1.2) +
  geom_point(aes(color = color_group), size = 4) +

  scale_color_manual(values = c("Power Model" = "steelblue", "Categorical" = "darkgreen"),
                     name = "Effect Type") +
  labs(
    title = "Traditional Forest Plot: Covariate Effect Parameters",
    subtitle = "Point estimates with 95% confidence intervals",
    x = "Parameter Estimate (θ)",
    y = "Covariate Effect"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 11),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  
  geom_text(aes(label = clinical_effect), 
            x = 1.5, hjust = 0, size = 3, color = "black") +
  
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-0.8, 2.5), 
                     breaks = seq(-0.5, 1.5, 0.5)) +
  
  # Add "No Effect" label
  annotate("text", x = 0.05, y = 7.5, label = "No Effect", 
           angle = 0, vjust = -0.5, color = "red", size = 4)

print(p_forest)

The covariate effects demonstrate varying degrees of clinical significance:

Covariate effects on Exposure

  • HV effect on CL : the patient population has 14.5% lower clearance than healthy volunteers, which would in increased AUC
  • ALT effect on CL: Impaired liver function was associated with lower clearance, which would result in increased AUC
  • Other covariate effects on Cmax and AUC may be quantified with simulations

6 Summary

  • Conduct simulations to assess optimal PK sampling times and evaluate covariate effects on Cmax and AUC

  • Subjects with decreased hepatic function demonstrated lower clearance. Exclude patients with decreased hepatic function until after safefty is confirmed with PK modeling and/or hepatic study.
